mappings.rationale: Directly covers the historical development of mathematics from ancient through modern times, the central topic of section A.
Provides an overview of mathematical concepts and foundations, relevant to both the historical development (A) and foundational questions (B) of this section.
Section A.2.a covers numeral systems from Egyptian to positional systems, and section B.2 discusses cardinal number and set theory foundations.
Section A.2.e covers the history of algebra from Babylonian through modern European developments, including the evolution of algebraic equations.
Section A.2.d covers the historical development of geometry from ancient through modern forms, including non-Euclidean geometry, projective geometry, and topology.
Trigonometry is part of the broader historical development of geometry and analysis covered in section A.2.d.
Section A.3 covers the historical development of probability theory, including the abstract calculus of probability and alternative views of probability.
Section A.3.b covers the development of mathematical statistics, including stochastic processes and control theory.
The concept of infinity is central to the foundations of mathematics (section B), including set theory, Cantor's work, and the crisis after 1900.
Section B.3 covers the crisis in foundations after 1900, including the paradoxes and the metamathematical method that Gödel's theorems epitomize.
Section B.3 discusses Logicism, Intuitionism, and Formalism as alternative philosophical positions on the foundations of mathematics, deeply connected to logic.
Russell's Logicism and the crisis in foundations (section B.3.c) are central to this section's coverage of the foundations of mathematics.
Section A.2.c covers the evolution of computational devices from the abacus to electronic digital computers, directly relevant to this VSI.
Section A.2.c.iii traces the evolution of digital devices from the abacus to modern computers, which is the history of computing.
Leibniz co-developed calculus (section A.1.b.i) and contributed to the foundations of mathematical reasoning, both central to this section.
Section A.1.b.i specifically names Newton as co-discoverer of calculus in the 17th century, a landmark in the history of mathematics.
Number theory and its historical development from ancient through modern times is part of the broader mathematical history covered in this section.
Section A.1.a.ii covers Greek and Hellenistic mathematics, which is part of the broader scientific achievements of the ancient Greek world.
Combinatorics is part of the broader historical development of mathematical branches covered in section A.
Fractals represent a 20th-century development in geometry and analysis mentioned in the modern mathematical developments of section A.1.b.iii.
Section A.2.d.vi lists topology as a modern idea in geometry, part of the historical development of mathematical concepts.
The development of calculus and analysis is a major thread in the history of mathematics covered in sections A.1.b.i through A.1.b.iii.
The relationship between pure and applied mathematics is part of the broader historical development covered in section A, including contributions from physical science.
